Ps 22:1-31.pericope_label: My God, My God — Lament and Vindication
Ps 22:1-31.literary_note: Psalm 22 is a full lament-to-praise psalm (a 'reversal psalm') with a clean two-part structure: vv.1-21a (lament: abandonment, mockery, encirclement by enemies), vv.21b-31 (praise: God has heard; universal proclamation). The hinge is v.21b: 'you have answered me' — abrupt, with no narrative of rescue. The psalmist praises before seeing the rescue, trusting the divine answer. The NT citations cluster in the passion narratives: Matt 27:35, 39, 43, 46.

key_terms.note: עֲזַבְתַּנִי (you have forsaken me, v.1): The sharpest cry of abandonment in the Psalter. This is not theological statement (God has not actually forsaken) but existential experience from within suffering. Jesus' citation from the cross (Matt 27:46) uses the Aramaic (Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani) — the entire Psalm is activated by citing its opening line.

בָּטַח (trusted, vv.4-5,9): The ancestors trusted and were not disappointed — trust is the thread connecting Israel's history to the psalmist's hope. The alternation between memory of God's faithfulness and present experience of abandonment is the psalm's emotional engine.

יִרְאוּ (they will see, v.27): The universal scope of the psalm's conclusion — all families of the nations, all prosperous and dying, future generations — will see and come to God. A single lament becomes a missional proclamation.

cultural_notes: Lament as prayer form: Israel's Psalter is 40% lament — the largest single category. Ancient and modern Christians have often suppressed lament as lack of faith. The Psalms authorize honest cry to God as the faithful response to suffering, not its opposite.

Passion narrative typology: Matthew 27 cites Psalm 22 extensively: the dividing of garments (v.18/Matt 27:35), the mocking crowds (v.7-8/Matt 27:39, 43), the cry of dereliction (v.1/Matt 27:46). This is not proof-texting but typological reading: David's suffering as a patterned foreshadowing of the Messiah's.

Second Temple interpretation: Some Qumran and Targumic texts identify the 'poor man' of Psalm 22 with the righteous sufferer awaiting divine vindication. The psalm was already understood as eschatologically pregnant.
intertextual_connections: Matt 27:46 — Jesus' cry from the cross. The whole psalm is activated: abandonment in vv.1-21a and vindication in vv.21b-31.
Heb 2:12 — quotes Ps 22:22 LXX: 'I will proclaim your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.' Applied to the risen Christ declaring God's name to the church.
Ps 69 — parallel lament structure; also heavily cited in the passion narratives (John 19:28-29 / Ps 69:21; John 2:17 / Ps 69:9).
Ps 22:1-31.synthesis: Psalm 22 is the paradigm of the movement from desolation to vindication that runs through the entire Psalter and reaches its apex in the passion and resurrection of Christ. The psalm does not resolve suffering intellectually — it resolves it liturgically: through the practice of continued address to God ('my God... you have not despised the affliction of the afflicted... but has heard'). The NT authors saw this as more than analogy: the pattern of David's suffering and vindication is the pattern of the Messiah's, which is in turn the pattern of all who suffer with and in Christ. Psalm 22 thus becomes both the passion script (for the Gospels) and the resurrection promise (for Hebrews 2). The Christian use of this psalm is not projection but pattern recognition — the same God who answered the psalmist has answered Christ, and through Christ, the whole congregation of the afflicted.

tradition_map.patristic: Origen devoted extensive commentary to Psalm 22 as Christological prophecy. The Seven Last Words tradition (Good Friday homilies) makes Psalm 22:1 the most-commented verse in Christian devotional history.
tradition_map.reformation: Calvin saw Psalm 22 as David speaking 'in the person of Christ' — the principle of sensus duplex: the psalm has a literal historical meaning and a fuller Christological meaning.
modern_debates: Does Jesus' citation indicate despair or trust? Luther and many Reformers argued that citing v.1 from the cross was a real cry of genuine forsakenness — not performative. Others argue: anyone familiar with the Psalter would know the whole psalm, including its confident ending in vindication.

Typological vs. predictive reading: Is Psalm 22 direct prediction (David somehow saw Christ's passion) or typological (David's experience follows the pattern the Messiah would supremely fulfill)? Both are defensible; most contemporary scholars prefer typological.

Ps 110:1-7.pericope_label: The LORD Says to My Lord — Priest-King at God's Right Hand
Ps 110:1-7.literary_note: Psalm 110 is the most-cited OT text in the NT (used over 20 times). It is a royal psalm addressed to a Davidic king, in two oracles: vv.1-3 (royal investiture: the king seated at God's right hand, enemies as footstool, rule among enemies) and vv.4-7 (priestly oracle: the king is a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek). The combination of royal and priestly offices in one figure was eschatologically freighted from the beginning.

key_terms.note: יְהוָה אָמַר לַאדֹנִי (the LORD says to my Lord, v.1): David's composition ('of David' — taken as author in the NT, cf. Mark 12:36) is addressed to 'my Lord' (ʾadônî) — the king's lord, not his equal or subordinate. The two Lords of v.1 provide the basis for Jesus' question (Matt 22:41-46): if David calls him 'Lord,' how is he merely David's son?

מַלְכִּי צֶדֶק (Melchizedek, v.4): The mysterious king-priest of Gen 14:18-20 who blessed Abraham. The oracle declares the Davidic king to be a priest 'in the order of Melchizedek' — a non-Aaronic, non-Levitical priesthood, superior (Heb 7:4-10) to the Levitical by virtue of blessing Abraham.

לִימִינִי (at my right hand, v.1): The position of supreme honor and authority in the ancient Near East — the king's regent. To be at God's right hand is to share in divine sovereign rule. The NT universally applies this to the ascended, reigning Christ (Acts 2:33-34; Heb 1:3; 1 Pet 3:22).

cultural_notes: Melchizedek background: Gen 14 presents Melchizedek as king of Salem and priest of El Elyon (God Most High). He is not an Israelite, not a Levite — he exists outside the Mosaic system entirely. This makes his priesthood both more ancient and (by the logic of Hebrews 7) more fundamental.

Right hand in the ANE: Being at the sovereign's right hand was political reality, not metaphor. It designated co-regency, authority to act in the king's name, and inheritance of the kingdom. Applied to Christ's ascension, it is the NT's primary political claim: Jesus is the world's true Lord.
intertextual_connections: Gen 14:18-20 — Melchizedek's first appearance. Abraham pays tithes to him — the original ranking that Hebrews 7 uses to establish Melchizedek's superiority to the Levitical order.
Matt 22:41-46 — Jesus cites Ps 110:1 to confound the Pharisees: 'If David calls him Lord, how is he his son?' The Christological argument turns on the two Lords.
Heb 5-7 — extended application of Ps 110:4 to Christ's priesthood. Jesus is the Melchizedekian priest who offers himself once-for-all.
Acts 2:34-35 — Peter's Pentecost sermon quotes Ps 110:1 as the key text explaining the resurrection and ascension.
Ps 110:1-7.synthesis: Psalm 110 is the NT's single most important OT proof-text — cited more than any other. It provides the framework for understanding Christ's present status (reigning at God's right hand), his priestly role (Melchizedekian, eternal, once-for-all), and his coming victory (enemies as footstool). The psalm holds together two roles that Israel's constitution strictly separated — king (Davidic/Judah) and priest (Levitical/Aaron). By declaring the Davidic king a Melchizedekian priest, it opens the conceptual space for a messiah who combines both offices — the king-priest who both rules and intercedes. The NT authors exploited this with precision: Jesus rules from the Father's right hand now (1 Cor 15:25), intercedes as high priest now (Heb 7:25), and will put all enemies under his feet at his return (1 Cor 15:26).

tradition_map.patristic: Justin Martyr used Ps 110:1 as his primary proof-text for Christ's pre-existence and divine status. The two Lords were the strongest OT evidence for the Son's deity.
tradition_map.reformation: Calvin: Psalm 110 is 'the key to the kingdom of Christ.' The Melchizedek typology shows Christ's priesthood supersedes Moses.
modern_debates: Davidic authorship: Modern scholarship is divided on whether Psalm 110 was composed by David or for David (as royal hymn). The NT (Mark 12:36; Acts 2:34) takes Davidic authorship as the basis of the argument — if that premise fails, the argument changes.

Targum Jonathan reinterpretation: The Targum renders 'my Lord' as 'my God' and gives the Psalm a military interpretation, avoiding the royal figure who is David's superior. This avoidance suggests the Psalm was already being read in ways that implied divine status for the Messiah.
